saida_bruta:
There was nothing gigantic in the appearance of the man beginning to wake on the sleeping porch of a Dutch Colonial house in Floral Heights, Zenith’s most fashionable residential district.

His name was George F. Babbitt. In April 1920, he was forty-six years old. He produced nothing in particular—neither butter, shoes, nor poetry—but he was adept at selling houses for more than their buyers could afford.

His large head was pink, his brown hair sparse and dry. In sleep his face looked boyish, despite the wrinkles and the red marks left by his spectacles along his nose. He was not fat, but he was exceedingly well fed; his cheeks were soft and full, and the smooth hand resting helplessly on the khaki blanket was slightly swollen. He looked prosperous, thoroughly married, and wholly unromantic. The sleeping porch itself was equally unromantic, overlooking a substantial elm, two respectable lawns, a concrete drive, and a corrugated-iron garage. Yet Babbitt was once more dreaming of the fairy child, a dream more romantic than scarlet pagodas beside a silver sea.
